history: The client has a pattern of forming close relationships with friends who are experiencing significant stressors. Currently, one friend is facing academic pressures and parenting an autistic child. The client is involved in supporting multiple friends who are distressed at the same time. There is an implied sense of responsibility for others' well-being and a tendency to become emotionally invested in friends' problems.
intermediate_belief: If my friends are struggling and I can't help them, then I am failing as a friend. I must always be available and effective in supporting those I care about. If I don't do enough, I am letting people down.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't keep up with everyone's needs, it means I'm not good enough and things will never get better.
coping_strategies: The client tries to organize and control their environment (e.g., cleaning, straightening up) to manage internal chaos. They may selectively focus on certain aspects of conversations to avoid feeling overwhelmed. The client tends to over-involve themselves in friends' problems, sometimes neglecting their own needs, and may withdraw or procrastinate when feeling emotionally drained.
situation: Multiple friends are distressed at the same time, and the patient is involved in supporting them.
auto_thought: I'm not doing enough to help everyone. If I can't fix their problems, I'm letting them down.
behavior: I spend extra time trying to help my friends, even when I feel exhausted. I try to organize things around me to feel more in control. Sometimes I withdraw or avoid my own tasks because I'm so focused on their needs.
